SECTION 1206 STATIONARY FUEL CELL POWER SYSTEMS

1206.1 General. Stationary fuel cell power systems in new and existing occupancies shall comply with this section.

Exception: The temporary use of a fuel cellpowered electric vehicle to power a Group R-3 or R-4 building while parked shall comply with Section 1206.14.

❖ ❖ This section deals with the regulation of stationary fuel cell power systems based on the fact that they are located in buildings, and the potential hazards they pose need to be addressed. Fuel cell power systems are being

SBC 801-CC-2024

1011

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

used in ever-increasing numbers to meet facility energy needs. Stationary fuel cell power systems generate power through an electrochemical process that combines hydrogen and oxygen to produce electricity. The hydrogen comes from a direct hydrogen source or from any hydrocarbon fuel such as natural gas, gasoline, diesel, or methanol if the fuel cell power system includes integral reforming. The oxygen comes from air around the fuel cell. There are several types of fuel cell systems that require different approval processes. These will be discussed further in Section 1206.3.
The exception points out the allowance in Section 1206.14 to temporarily provide power to a Group R-3 or R-4 occupancy. This allowance is also provided in SBC 1101/1102 for similar structures. See the commentary to Section 1206.14.
1206.2 Permits. Permits shall be obtained for stationary fuel cell power systems as set forth in Section 105.6.9.
❖ Section 105.6.9 requires a permit to allow the fire department to become familiar with and sign off on the safety of such systems.
1206.3 Equipment. Stationary fuel cell power systems shall comply with the following:
1. Prepackaged fuel cell power systems shall be listed and labeled in accordance with CSA FC 1.
2. The modules and components in a preengineered fuel cell power system shall be listed and labeled in accordance with CSA FC 1 and interconnected to complete the assembly of the system at the job site in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions and the module and component listings.
3. Field-fabricated fuel cell power systems shall be approved based on a review of the technical report provided in accordance with Section 104.8.2. The report shall be prepared by and bear the stamp of a registered design professional and shall include:
3.1. A fire risk evaluation.
3.2. An evaluation demonstrating that modules and components in the fuel cell power system comply with applicable requirements in CSA FC 1.
3.3. Documentation of the fuel cell power system’s compliance with applicable

NFPA 2 and NFPA 853 construction requirements.
❖ Three types of fuel cell power systems are defined in Chapter 2. They include:
• Prepackaged fuel cell power systems.
• Preengineering fuel cell power systems.
• Field-fabricated fuel cell power systems.
Each of these systems has a different approach to approval. Prepackaged systems come as a complete unit and simply need to be listed and labeled in accordance with CSA FC1. These systems are ready for installation right from the factory, whereas preengineered and field-fabricated systems require more approvals during installation. The requirements for listing and labeling to CSA FC1 remove the burden to the Fire official of determining compliance with NFPA 853, which is a standard for the design, construction, and installation of stationary fuel cell power systems very similar in content to CSA FC1. Preengineered systems fall between prepackaged and field fabricated. The modules and components are listed and labeled, and the system is approved but must be assembled at the job site. Again, the listing essentially demonstrates compliance with NFPA 853. In addition to listing and labeling preengineered fuel cell power systems, the manufacturer’s instructions must be followed. Field fabricated is a system completely assembled at the job site and the modules are not listed or labeled. Instead, they need to meet several criteria, including demonstrating compliance with CSA FC1. This option allows for newer technologies to be used that have not been designed yet as prepackaged or preengineered. In addition to compliance with CSA FC 1, a risk analysis is necessary. Additionally, hydrogen, a common fuel used for fuel cell power systems, must meet the requirements in NFPA 2 and NFPA 853.
ANSI/CSA FC 1 is used to investigate and list the stationary fuel cells covered by this section. The construction and performance requirements in that standard address a variety of hazards, including mechanical, electrical, thermal, malfunction, erroneous human intervention and environmental. NFPA 853, Standard for the Installation of Stationary Fuel Cell Power Systems, includes requirements for the design, construction and installation of stationary fuel cell power systems.
1206.4 Installation. Stationary fuel cell power systems shall be installed and maintained in accordance with SBC 401 and NFPA 853, the

SBC 801-CC-2024

1012

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

manufacturer’s installation instructions, and the listing. Stationary fuel cell power systems fueled by hydrogen shall be installed and maintained in accordance with NFPA 2 and SBC 401, the manufacturer’s installation instructions and the listing.

❖ This section is provided to ensure that such systems are properly installed and maintained, regardless of type. Several standards are called up for this process.

1206.5 Residential use. Stationary fuel cell power systems shall not be installed in Group R-3 and R4 buildings, or dwelling units associated with Group R-2 buildings unless they are specifically listed for residential use.

Exception: The temporary use of a fuel cellpowered electric vehicle to power a Group R-3 or R-4 building while parked shall comply with Section 1206.14.

❖ Systems are listed for both residential and nonresidential use based on the requirements in NFPA 853 and CSA FC1. This section is provided to ensure the appropriate stationary fuel cell power system is installed.

The exception acknowledges the allowance in Section 1206.14 that would allow the use of a vehicle for temporary power for Group R-3 and R-4 occupancies. See the commentary to Section 1206.14.

1206.6 Indoor installations. Stationary fuel cell power systems installed in indoor locations shall comply with Sections 1206.6 through 1206.6.2. For purposes of this section, an indoor location includes a roof and 50 percent or greater enclosing walls.

❖ Systems are listed for either indoor or outdoor use. The 50-percent wall limitation is from NFPA 853, Section 3.3.15.2. This criterion is necessary to differentiate which installations are considered indoor versus outdoor, as the requirements vary.

1206.6.1

Listed. Stationary fuel cell power

systems installed indoors shall be specifically listed

and labeled for indoor use.

❖ The purpose of this section is to ensure that indoor systems are listed and labeled appropriately for indoor use. This makes it clear to the Fire official that what is being installed is appropriate for the location and use without requiring verification through additional documentation.

1206.6.2

Separation. Rooms containing

stationary fuel cell power systems shall be

separated from the following occupancies by fire barriers or horizontal assemblies, or both, constructed in accordance with SBC 201.
1. Group B, F, M, S and U occupancies by 1hour fire-resistance-rated construction.
2. Group A, E, I and R occupancies by 2-hour fire-resistance-rated construction.
Exception: Stationary fuel cell power systems with an aggregate rating less than 50 kW shall not be required to be separated from other occupancies provided that the systems comply with Section 9.3 of NFPA 853.
❖ These systems are essentially like an incidental use to the building and separations are provided for consistency with that concept. The separations are similar to those required for ESS in Section 1207.7.4. Note that Group B, F, M, S and U occupancies would require only a 1-hour separation, whereas a 2-hour separation would be required for ESS. Systems less than 50 kW do not require additional separation, as the risk they pose is low.
1206.7 Vehicle impact protection. Where stationary fuel cell power systems are subject to impact by a motor vehicle, vehicle impact protection shall be provided in accordance with Section 312.
❖ The provisions in this section protect the power system from physical damage to avoid a malfunction of the equipment and the possible resulting hazards. A good example may be a fuel cell in a warehouse space where forklifts are being used (see commentary, Section 312).
1206.8 Outdoor installation. Stationary fuel cell power systems located outdoors shall be separated by not less than 1.5 m from the following:
1. Lot lines.
2. Public ways.
3. Buildings.
4. Stored combustible materials.
5. Hazardous materials.
6. High-piled stock.
7. Any portion of a designated means of egress system.
8. Other exposure hazards.
❖ The exposures are different for installations on the exterior, as there are other exposures such

SBC 801-CC-2024

1013

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

as adjacent buildings. The separations are based on, but not identical to, requirements in NFPA 853, Section 5.1.1.
1206.9 Fuel supply. The design, location and installation of the fuel supply for stationary fuel cell power systems shall comply with Chapter 53, Chapter 58 and SBC 1201, based on the particular fuel being supplied to the system.
❖ This section does not address the fuel supply but instead provides references to the appropriate requirements. The primary fuels are hydrogen and CNG.
1206.10Manual shutoff. Access to a manual shutoff valve shall be provided for the fuel piping within 1.8 m of any fuel storage tank serving the fuel cell and within 1.8 m of the power system. If the fuel tank and the stationary fuel cell power system are less than 3.6 m apart, a single shutoff valve shall be permitted. If the stationary fuel cell power system is located indoors, the shutoff valve shall be located outside of the room in which the system is installed, unless otherwise approved by the Fire official.
❖ Manual shutoff requirements are based on, but not identical to, requirements in NFPA 853, Section 6.4. Note that Section 6.4 deals with hydrogen specifically, but this section would deal with any fuel being used for the fuel cell. Fuel cells generate electricity but need a fuel source such as hydrogen or CNG. Therefore, shutoff valves must be provided in case of malfunction of the fuel supply at the fuel cell to avoid further hazards.
This section essentially is intended to provide two shutoff valves that are located within 1.8 m of the fuel cell and within 1.8 m of the fuel source. Some flexibility is given to systems where the fuel source and cell are less than 3.6 m apart and thus can share a single shutoff valve. Indoor fuel cells must provide shutoff valves outside the room in which the fuel cell is located. This reduces the risk of entering a space with a flammable gas.
1206.11Ventilation and exhaust. Ventilation and exhaust for stationary fuel cell power systems shall be provided in accordance with NFPA 853.
❖ Ventilation, exhaust, fire suppression and gas detection are important parts of a safe installation, and NFPA 853 is referenced for convenience. Chapter 7 of NFPA 853 addresses ventilation and exhaust. Section 7.1.1 of NFPA 853 exempts fuel cell power systems installed outdoors and listed prepackaged or preengineered and matched modular fuel cell power systems that are

sealed, provide direct ventilation and exhaust in accordance with the listing and manufacturer. Outdoor installations have more air circulation to reduce the hazard of flammable gas buildup, and preengineered and prepacked installations design ventilation and exhaust into the system. The provisions focus on having separate ventilation and exhaust for the power systems and to interlock the systems in case of failure of the exhaust system.
1206.12Fire protection. Fire protection systems for stationary fuel cell power system installations shall be provided in accordance with NFPA 853.
❖ For outdoor installations, NFPA 853 essentially requires appropriate fire hydrants be available to fight a fire. Fuel cell power systems that store flammable and combustible liquids for a fuel source require a minimum of 250 gpm for 2 hours. Where flammable and combustible liquids are not used, the existing yard or city hydrant system would be sufficient. In the case of this code, compliance with Section 507 is sufficient. Where hydrants are not available, NFPA 853 offers the option of undertaking a risk evaluation to address the protection needs.
Indoor installations in accordance with NFPA 853 require automatic fire protection systems within the liquid fuel pump rooms. Such protection systems need to be interlocked with the fuel cell power system to shut down the system upon activation.
1206.13Gas detection systems. Stationary fuel cell power systems shall be provided with a gas detection system. Detection shall be provided in approved locations in the fuel cell power system enclosure, the exhaust system or the room that encloses the fuel cell power system. The system shall be designed to activate at a flammable gas concentration of not more than 25 percent of the lower flammable limit (LFL).
❖ Gas detection system requirements include detection locations from UL 853 and activation criteria that are consistent with the requirements of this code. Gas detection is critical with fuels such as hydrogen and is intended for fuel cell power systems using such fuels. The necessary locations for the detection devices will vary based on each installation and system; therefore, the term “approved” is used for their location. Note that NFPA 853 states that the detectors be located based on “leakage sources and fuel type.” The intent is not to provide gas detection when liquid fuels are being used. NFPA 853 does require leak detection for liquid fuels.

SBC 801-CC-2024

1014

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

1206.13.1 System activation. The activation of the gas detection system shall automatically:
1. Close valves between the gas supply and the fuel cell power system.
2. Shut down the fuel cell power system.
3. Initiate local audible and visible alarms in approved locations.
❖ The initiation of the gas detection creates three specific actions that shut off the gas supply, shut down the fuel cell and provide at least a local alarm to alert someone of the hazard. Note that such actions occur once the gas has reached 25 percent of the lower flammable limit (LFL). Again, gas detection is intended for liquefied or gaseous fuels.
1206.14Group R-3 and R-4 fuel cell vehicle energy storage system use. The temporary use of the dwelling unit owner or occupant′s fuel cellpowered electric vehicle to power a Group R-3 or R-4 dwelling while parked in an attached or detached garage or outside shall comply with the vehicle manufacturer′s instructions and SBC 401.
❖ This section permits the use of fuel cellpowered vehicles as a temporary power supply for smaller residential buildings, essentially one- and two-family homes and townhouses. This same allowance is also found in SBC 1101/1102. The vehicles must belong to an owner or occupant of the dwelling unit, not a third party. (Thirdparty applications of mobile ESS are separately regulated by Section 1207.) Additionally, the use of fuel cellpowered vehicles must comply with the vehicle manufacturer′s instructions and SBC 401 to provide for an appropriate level of safety. The vehicle manufacturer′s information covers the approved electrical connection on the vehicle itself in accordance with government standards, and SBC 401 covers the necessary requirements for the electrical connections to the dwelling. Vehicles capable of being used in this manner are more than a convenience issue; in times of natural disasters with associated power outages, the ability to utilize the energy provided by the owner/occupant′s personal vehicle could be critical.
SECTION 1207 ELECTRICAL ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS (ESS)
1207.1 General. The provisions in this section are applicable to stationary and mobile electrical energy storage systems (ESS).
Exception: ESS in Group R-3 and R-4 occupancies shall comply with Section 1207.11.

❖ Advancements in battery technologies have introduced a new generation of battery technologies, such as lithium-ion and flow batteries, each with advantages and potential hazards. Examples such as the events in Surprise, Arizona, in 2019 where four fire fighters were seriously injured due to an explosion involving an ESS installation are a reminder of why ESS need to be regulated. That particular installation did not have the benefit of more current code requirements (https://ulfirefighter-safety.org/posts/fourfirefighters-injured-in-lithium-ion-batteryenergy-storage-system-explosion.html).

This section has been expanded to address the changing environment of ESS. “Energy storage systems (ESS)” are defined in Chapter 2, and the scope of these provisions as noted cover both stationary installations and mobile ESS.

1207.1.1

Scope. ESS having capacities

exceeding the values shown in Table 1207.1.1 shall

comply with this section.

❖ As discussed, Section 1207 deals with ESS,

whether stationary or mobile. Due to the

changes, advances and expanding use of

ESS, the requirements have dramatically

increased in scope from what was once found

in this code. Now the provisions address not

only ESS provided for standby and emergency

power, but also for functions such as load

shedding and load sharing. It should be noted

that the code does not mandate many of the

applications using these systems but instead is

simply providing the necessary safety

mechanisms to prevent and mitigate fires and

explosions that could result due to the inherent

hazards associated with the technologies.

Providing protection for these new

technologies and the huge amounts of energy

they store is something that needs to be

addressed through research, fire and fault

condition testing, and the development of

effective safety standards. Research is

ongoing as to how best protect these

technologies, but many advances from current

research results are incorporated in these

provisions, which are very similar to NFPA 855.

The provisions still accommodate the lead-acid

battery systems used by the

telecommunications

industry

with

technologyspecific

criteria

relatively

unchanged from older editions of the code, as

such systems continue to have a good safety

track record.

The provisions of Section 1207 apply only to systems that meet the minimum thresholds listed in Table 1207.1.1. Smaller systems would not require regulation by the code.

SBC 801-CC-2024

1015

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

1207.1.2

Permits. Permits shall be obtained

for ESS as follows:

1. Construction permits shall be obtained for stationary ESS installations and for mobile ESS charging and storage installations covered by Section 1207.10.1. Permits shall be obtained in accordance with Section 105.6.5.

2. Operational permits shall be obtained for stationary ESS installations and for mobile ESS deployment operations covered by Section 1207.10.3. Permits shall be obtained in accordance with Section 105.5.14.

❖ Construction permits are essential as they facilitate the necessary review process for such ESS by the Fire official. These systems can be used in many different applications, each with their own specific hazards to be addressed based on those applications. This section also requires operational permits for both stationary and mobile ESS so that the fire department is aware of potential ongoing hazards in the community.

1207.1.2.1 Communication

utilities.

Operational permits shall not be required for lead-

acid and nickel-cadmium battery systems at

facilities under the exclusive control of

communications utilities that comply with NFPA

76 and operate at less than 50 voltage alternating

current (VAC) and 60 voltage direct current (VDC).

❖ This section allows operational permits to be omitted for a proven technology already appropriately regulated by communication utilities.

1207.1.3

Construction documents.

The following information shall be provided with

the permit application:

1. Location and layout diagram of the room or area in which the ESS is to be installed.

2. Details on the hourly fire-resistance ratings of assemblies enclosing the ESS.

3. The quantities and types of ESS to be installed.

4. Manufacturer′s specifications, ratings and listings of each ESS.

5. Description of energy (battery) management systems and their operation.

6. Location and content of required signage.

7. Details on fire suppression, smoke or fire detection, thermal management, ventilation,

exhaust and deflagration venting systems, if provided.

8. Support arrangement associated with the installation, including any required seismic restraint.

9. A commissioning plan complying with Section 1207.2.1.

10. A decommissioning plan complying with Section 1207.2.3.

❖ Extensive documentation is required to obtain a permit. This helps the Fire official understand the extent and location of the hazard and to better be able to determine compliance with Section 1207. This information is also necessary for planning purposes. These documents specifically require both a commissioning and decommissioning plan, which is critical to overall safety. ESS must be properly installed and tested but also cannot simply be abandoned because of the energy and hazards that may remain.

1207.1.4

Hazard mitigation analysis. A

failure modes and effects analysis (FMEA) or other

approved hazard mitigation analysis shall be

provided in accordance with Section 104.8.2 under

any of the following conditions:

1. Where ESS technologies not specifically identified in Table 1207.1.1 are provided.

2. More than one ESS technology is provided in a room or enclosed area where there is a potential for adverse interaction between technologies.

3. Where allowed as a basis for increasing maximum allowable quantities. See Section 1207.5.2.

❖ This section requires a hazard mitigation analysis in the form of a failure modes and effects analysis (FMEA) for certain situations. This specifically references Section 104.8.2 for proper analysis and documentation requirements. Item 1 addresses new technologies. This allows for flexibility for innovative technology with appropriate analysis and documentation.

ESS applications are being used in very different ways than in the past, and in some cases, multiple types of systems may be located in a single area within a building. Item 2 requires this situation to be addressed through a hazard analysis to make sure that adverse conditions caused by mixing technologies are not realized.

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

In terms of Item 3, in some cases the capacity of the system surpasses that of Section 1207.5.2. To apply the requirements of Section 1207 to these larger systems, a hazard mitigation analysis is necessary.
1207.1.4.1 Fault condition. The hazard mitigation analysis shall evaluate the consequences of the following failure modes. Only single failure modes shall be considered.
1. A thermal runaway condition in a single ESS rack, module or unit.
2. Failure of any battery (energy) management system.
3. Failure of any required ventilation or exhaust system.
4. Voltage surges on the primary electric supply.
5. Short circuits on the load side of the ESS.
6. Failure of the smoke detection, fire detection, fire suppression or gas detection system.
7. Required spill neutralization not being provided or failure of a required secondary containment system.
❖ This section sets the criteria as to what failure modes need to be evaluated for the analysis required in Section 1207.1.4. The provisions are only asking that a single failure be addressed for each scenario, similar to the fact that the building and fire codes have been written with the concept of a single fire start as a reasonable level of design.
1207.1.4.2 Analysis approval. The Fire official is authorized to approve the hazardous mitigation analysis provided that the consequences of the hazard mitigation analysis demonstrate:
1. Fires will be contained within unoccupied ESS rooms or areas for the minimum duration of the fire-resistance-rated separations identified in Section 1207.7.4.
2. Fires in occupied work centers will be detected in time to allow occupants within the room or area to safely evacuate.
3. Toxic and highly toxic gases released during fires will not reach concentrations in excess of the IDLH level in the building or adjacent means of egress routes during the time deemed necessary to evacuate occupants from any affected area.

4. 4.Flammable gases released from ESS during charging, discharging and normal operation will not exceed 25 percent of their lower flammability limit (LFL).

5. 5.Flammable gases released from ESS during fire, overcharging and other abnormal conditions will be controlled through the use of ventilation of the gases, preventing accumulation, or by deflagration venting.

❖ A hazardous mitigation analysis is required under certain circumstances, identified in Section 1207.1.4, to address uncertainties associated with new technologies and configurations. This section describes the acceptance criteria to judge acceptability of the ESS, whereas Section 1207.1.4.1 identifies which failures are to be analyzed.

1207.1.4.3 Additional protection measures. Construction, equipment and systems that are required for the ESS to comply with the hazardous mitigation analysis, including but not limited to those specifically described in Section 1207, shall be installed, maintained and tested in accordance with nationally recognized standards and specified design parameters.

❖ Essentially, this section ensures that the ESS are designed and installed in accordance with industry practice and work with the design parameters approved for the unique installation.

1207.1.5

Large-scale fire test. Where

required elsewhere in Section 1207, large-scale fire

testing shall be conducted on a representative ESS

in accordance with UL 9540A. The testing shall be

conducted or witnessed and reported by an

approved testing laboratory and show that a fire

involving one ESS will not propagate to an adjacent

ESS, and where installed within buildings, enclosed

areas and walk-in units will be contained within the

room, enclosed area or walk-in unit for a duration

equal to the fire-resistance rating of the room

separation specified in Section 1207.7.4. The test

report shall be provided to the Fire official for

review and approval in accordance with Section

104.8.2.

❖ This section provides the details required if testing of new and innovative approaches to ESS are used. This is based on large-scale fire and fault condition testing with specific criteria for success and failure. The UL 9540A Test Method was specifically developed to cover this testing. Since extinguishment of a fire involving a thermal runaway in an ESS is extremely difficult, if not impossible, the code

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

requirements are based on the concept of an ESS unit being completely consumed by fire without propagating to adjacent ESS. A largescale fire test is essential to documenting that unit-to-unit propagation will not occur as well as generating additional data for necessary risk assessments for varied installation locations and arrangements relative to exposures.

1207.1.6

Fire remediation. Where a fire or

other event has damaged the ESS and ignition or re-

ignition of the ESS is possible, the system owner,

agent or lessee shall take the following actions, at

their expense, to mitigate the hazard or remove

damaged equipment from the premises to a safe

location.

❖ This section addresses fire events involving lithium-ion battery systems, since lithium-ion battery fires have the potential to re-ignite hours or even days after initial extinguishment by the fire department , which cannot remain on the scene indefinitely until the fire-damaged ESS is safely removed from the premises. The fire remediation requirements, similar to fire watch requirements, make the owner responsible for sending mitigation personnel to the scene to take over the remediation process.

1207.1.6.1 Fire mitigation personnel. Where, in the opinion of the Fire official, it is essential for public safety that trained personnel be on-site to respond to possible ignition or re-ignition of a damaged ESS, the system owner, agent or lessee shall immediately dispatch one or more fire mitigation personnel to the premise, as required and approved, at their expense. These personnel shall remain on duty continuously after the fire department leaves the premise until the damaged energy storage equipment is removed from the premises, or earlier if the Fire official indicates the public safety hazard has been abated.

❖ This section sets up the personnel in advance so that if fire mitigation is required beyond the initial event, there are qualified personnel ready to be dispatched. It is important that the assigned personnel have the requisite level of knowledge to recognize and effectively respond to the specific hazards of the damaged ESS. This section clarifies who is responsible for providing this service. It also sets out that the owner is financially responsible for such services.

1207.1.6.2 Duties. On-duty fire mitigation personnel shall have the following responsibilities:

1. Keep a diligent watch for fires, obstructions to means of egress and other hazards.

2. Immediately contact the fire department if their assistance is needed to mitigate any hazards or extinguish fires.

3. Take prompt measures for remediation of hazards in accordance with the decommissioning plan per Section 1207.2.3.

4. Take prompt measures to assist in the evacuation of the public from the structures.

❖ This section simply describes the specific responsibilities of such fire mitigation personnel. This is similar to Section 403.11.1.2 for fire watch personnel.

1207.2 Commissioning,

decommissioning,

operation and maintenance. Commissioning,

decommissioning, operation and maintenance shall

be conducted in accordance with this section.

❖ This section covers commissioning, decommissioning, maintenance and testing requirements, which are important considerations for providing a safe, codecompliant installation and long-term successful performance.

1207.2.1

Commissioning. Commissioning

of newly installed ESS and existing ESS that have

been retrofitted, replaced or previously

decommissioned and are returning to service shall

be conducted prior to the ESS being placed in

service in accordance with a commissioning plan

that has been approved prior to initiating

commissioning. The commissioning plan shall

include the following:

1. A narrative description of the activities that will be accomplished during each phase of commissioning, including the personnel intended to accomplish each of the activities.

2. A listing of the specific ESS and associated components, controls and safety-related devices to be tested, a description of the tests to be performed and the functions to be tested.

3. Conditions under which all testing will be performed, which are representative of the conditions during normal operation of the system.

4. Documentation of the owner′s project requirements and the basis of design necessary to understand the installation and operation of the ESS.

5. Verification that required equipment and systems are installed in accordance with the approved plans and specifications.
6. Integrated testing for all fire and safety systems.
7. Testing for any required thermal management, ventilation or exhaust systems associated with the ESS installation.
8. Preparation and delivery of operation and maintenance documentation.
9. Training of facility operating and maintenance staff.
10. Identification and documentation of the requirements for maintaining system performance to meet the original design intent during the operation phase.
11. Identification and documentation of personnel who are qualified to service, maintain and decommission the ESS, and respond to incidents involving the ESS, including documentation that such service has been contracted for.
12. A decommissioning plan for removing the ESS from service, and from the facility in which it is located. The plan shall include details on providing a safe, orderly shutdown of energy storage and safety systems with notification to the building and fire officials prior to the actual decommissioning of the system. The decommissioning plan shall include contingencies for removing an intact operational ESS from service, and for removing an ESS from service that has been damaged by a fire or other event.
Exception: Commissioning shall not be required for leadacid and nickel-cadmium battery systems at facilities under the exclusive control of communications utilities that comply with NFPA 76 and operate at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC. A decommissioning plan shall be provided and maintained where required by the Fire official.
❖ The main intent of this section is to make sure that an ESS installation will perform as designed safely. The requirements of this section are best practice requirements that communicate how the system is to work and verify that it is in fact operating as designed. This process inherently reduces the risk of system failure. This includes the need to provide a plan for decommissioning from the very start to properly address the lifecycle of

the system.

1207.2.1.1 Initial acceptance testing. During the commissioning process an ESS shall be evaluated for proper operation in accordance with the manufacturer′s instructions and the commissioning plan prior to final approval.

❖ This section ensures that the verification process occurs and that such process is consistent with both the manufacturer′s instructions and established commissioning plan.

1207.2.1.2 Commissioning report. A report describing the results of the system commissioning, including the results of the initial acceptance testing required in Section 1207.2.1.1, shall be provided to the Fire official prior to final inspection and approval and maintained at an approved on-site location.

❖ ❖ This section requires that the commissioning process is documented prior to final approval. This report may be critical in the future and needs to be placed in an approved on-site location. The location of the commissioning report will vary and will need to be specific to each installation. It should be removed from any potentially hazardous location but needs to be convenient to the ESS installation. There is also the possibility it could be electronically available; however, this section does not go into that level of specificity.

1207.2.2

Operation and maintenance. An

operation and maintenance manual shall be

provided to both the ESS owner or their authorized

agent and the ESS operator before the ESS is put

into operation and shall include the following:

1. Manufacturer′s operation manuals and maintenance manuals for the entire ESS, or for each component of the system requiring maintenance, that clearly identify the required routine maintenance actions.

2. Name, address and phone number of a service agency that has been contracted to service the ESS and its associated safety systems.

3. Maintenance and calibration information, including wiring diagrams, control drawings, schematics, system programming instructions and control sequence descriptions, for all energy storage control systems.

4. Desired or field-determined control set points that are permanently recorded on

-

control drawings at control devices or, for digital control systems, in system programming instructions.

5. A schedule for inspecting and recalibrating all ESS controls.

6. A service record log form that lists the schedule for all required servicing and maintenance actions and space for logging such actions that are completed over time and retained on-site.

7. The ESS shall be operated and maintained in accordance with the manual and a copy of the manual shall be retained at an approved onsite location.

❖ This manual is part of the package of requirements that ensures proper operation and maintenance to reduce the likelihood of a failure. All the pertinent information is contained in this manual for ongoing operation, including the contact information for those who do maintenance or repairs on the system. The schedule for inspecting and recalibrating ESS controls is included along with a service log to ensure the system is properly maintained. Similar to the commissioning report, a copy of the manual needs to be located in an approved on-site location. See the commentary to Section 1207.2.1.2.

1207.2.2.1 Ongoing inspection and testing. Systems that monitor and protect the ESS installation shall be inspected and tested in accordance with the manufacturer′s instructions and the operation and maintenance manual. Inspection and testing records shall be maintained in the operation and maintenance manual.

❖ In addition to the ESS itself, systems such as gas detection, ventilation, smoke detection and automatic sprinklers also need to be maintained to assist in mitigating any failures. These records also need to be included in the operation and maintenance manual.

1207.2.3

Decommissioning. The fire

official shall be notified prior to the

decommissioning of an ESS. Decommissioning

shall be performed in accordance with the

decommissioning plan that includes the following:

1. A narrative description of the activities to be accomplished for removing the ESS from service, and from the facility in which it is located.

2. A listing of any contingencies for removing an intact operational ESS from service, and

for removing an ESS from service that has been damaged by a fire or other event.

❖ Decommissioning is as critical as commissioning when it comes to ESS. Such systems are subject to thermal runaway even when they are no longer in use. Therefore, they must be properly removed from service, which typically means removal from the facility. The requirements will vary based on if it is a functioning system or one that is damaged. Though the commissioning report is required to contain a decommissioning report for the ESS, in accordance with Section 1207.2.1, it is recognized that if decommissioning is due to damage from fire or other event, the specific details of safe decommissioning will be incident specific.

1207.3 Equipment. ESS equipment shall be in accordance with Sections 1207.3.1 through 1207.3.9.

❖ This section covers the ESS equipment requirements, including listings. This section includes requirements related to repairs, retrofits and replacements when systems need to be upgraded or serviced. Such guidance is necessary with rapidly changing technologies and the need to make sure that ESS operate as optimally as possible.

1207.3.1

Energy storage system listings.

ESS shall be listed in accordance with UL 9540.

Exception: Lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery

systems installed in facilities under the exclusive

control of communications utilities, and operating

at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC in accordance

with NFPA 76, are not required to be listed.

❖ Energy storage systems are required to be listed in accordance with UL 9540. This standard covers the listing of storage batteries and all the equipment associated with the system, including cabinets and prefabricated enclosures.

The exception recognizes the proven technology of lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery systems as operated through communications utilities that comply with NFPA 76.

1207.3.2

Equipment listing. Chargers,

inverters and energy storage management systems

shall be covered as part of the UL 9540 listing or

shall be listed separately.

❖ There are separate listings that address chargers, inverters and energy management systems where they are not part of the UL 9540 listing. If they are not part of the UL 9540 listing,

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

they must be addressed individually by specific listing standards.

1207.3.3

Utility interactive systems.

Inverters shall be listed and labeled in accordance

with UL 1741. Only inverters listed and labeled for

utility interactive system use and identified as

interactive shall be allowed to operate in parallel

with the electric utility power system to supply

power to common loads.

❖ This section requires a listing in accordance with UL 1741 where it is not part of the UL 9540 listing. An inverter is an electronic device that is capable of changing direct current (DC) to alternating current (AC). Inverters need to be specifically listed and labeled to avoid using the wrong type and subsequently leading to system failure. Note that this section also requires a specific listing for utility interactive systems. These types of inverters allow things like automatic transfer to enable the ESS to feed power back into the commercial power grid or disconnect from the commercial grid when it is supposed to be de-energized due to an outage. These types of inverters must be inspected by the electrical inspector and the power utility in most cases.

1207.3.4

Energy storage management

system. Where required by the ESS listing, an

approved energy storage management system that

monitors and balances cell voltages, currents and

temperatures within the manufacturer′s

specifications shall be provided. The system shall

disconnect electrical connections to the ESS or

otherwise place it in a safe condition if potentially

hazardous temperatures or other conditions such as

short circuits, over voltage or under voltage are

detected.

❖ An energy management system is an important safety system that monitors and takes actions on abnormal conditions that could lead to failures. These systems are critical as part of the risk reduction.

1207.3.5

Enclosures. Enclosures of ESS

shall be of noncombustible construction.

❖ This prevents other methods of fire spread from one ESS to another or other combustibles within a building.

1207.3.6

Repairs. Repairs of ESS shall only

be done by qualified personnel. Repairs with other

than identical parts shall be considered retrofitting

and comply with Section 1207.3.7. Repairs shall be

documented in the service records log.

❖ Several requirements are addressed in this section. The first is the necessary qualifications

of those undertaking the repairs. This is especially important given that these systems are complex and at risk of failure if the repairs are done incorrectly. Second, system innovations are happening quickly, and identical parts may not be available. To ensure compatibility in those cases, the replacement parts and equipment need to be addressed as a retrofit in accordance with Section 1207.3.7. This section requires all repairs to be documented.

1207.3.7

Retrofits. Retrofitting of an

existing ESS shall comply with the following:

1. A construction permit shall be obtained in accordance with Section 105.6.5.

2. New batteries, battery modules, capacitors and similar ESS components shall be listed.

3. Battery management and other monitoring systems shall be connected and installed in accordance with the manufacturer′s instructions.

4. The overall installation shall continue to comply with UL 9540 listing requirements, where applicable.

5. Systems that have been retrofitted shall be commissioned in accordance with Section 1207.2.1.

6. Retrofits shall be documented in the service records log.

❖ This section addresses practices to be followed when systems need to be upgraded or serviced. These systems are very sensitive to changes that could lead to thermal runaway, so clear requirements for retrofit need to be outlined. This includes obtaining a construction permit to notify the fire department a retrofit is occurring. In addition, batteries must be properly listed and the overall installation must comply with UL 9540. Finally, the system needs to be commissioned to demonstrate performance, and records of the process need to be provided.

1207.3.7.1 Retrofitting lead acid and nickel cadmium. Section 1207.3.7 shall not apply to retrofitting of lead-acid and nickel-cadmium batteries with other lead-acid and nickelcadmium batteries at facilities under the exclusive control of communications utilities that comply with NFPA 76 and operate at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC.

❖ ❖ These technologies, where small in capacity, are sufficiently regulated and have performed safely under the control of

communications utilities that comply with NFPA 76. This exception is technology specific; for example, if a lead-acid battery was replaced with a lithium-ion battery, that would not be considered a retrofit. The change in battery technology would require the system to be treated as new, and all appropriate requirements of Section 1207 would apply.

1207.3.8

Replacements. Replacements of

ESS shall be considered new ESS installations and

shall comply with the provisions of Section 1207 as

applicable to new ESS. The ESS being replaced

shall be decommissioned in accordance with

Section 1207.2.3.

❖ This section requires that replacement of ESS shall be addressed no differently than as new and Section 1207 would apply to this installation. Where an ESS system is being replaced, it is important that such systems are properly decommissioned since the existing system may still be energized or subject to thermal runaway.

1207.3.9

Reused and repurposed

equipment. Equipment and materials shall only be

reused or reinstalled as permitted in Section

104.8.1. Storage batteries previously used in other

applications, such as electric vehicle propulsion,

shall not be reused in applications regulated by

Chapter 12 unless approved by the Fire official and

unless the equipment is refurbished by a battery

refurbishing company approved in accordance with

UL 1974.

❖ This section allows Fire officials to regulate installations of repurposed electric vehicle batteries that are converted for ESS use in buildings. It is important to recognize that UL 1974 applies to the process used by the firm refurbishing the battery. It is not a listing of the battery; once refurbished, the use of such batteries must be in compliance with Section 1207, including battery and system listing requirements.

1207.4 General installations requirements. Stationary and mobile ESS shall comply with the requirements of Sections 1207.4.1 through 1207.4.12.

❖ This section is focused on a number of requirements that facilitate the safe operation of ESS such as proper electrical disconnects, clearances, seismic supports and separation from combustibles. These features are part of the package of safety measures that reduce risk of a failure, which may lead to hazardous conditions.

1207.4.1

Electrical disconnects. Where the

ESS disconnecting means is not within sight of the

main electrical service disconnecting means,

placards or directories shall be installed at the

location of the main electrical service disconnecting

means indicating the location of stationary storage

battery system disconnecting means in accordance

with SBC 401.

Exception: Electrical disconnects for lead-acid and nickelcadmium battery systems at facilities under the exclusive control of communications utilities and operating at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC shall be permitted to have electrical disconnects signage in accordance with NFPA 76.

❖ Stationary storage battery systems may be in various locations within buildings and may not be near the main service disconnect. Therefore, a way of communicating the disconnect for such systems is necessary at the main service disconnect. This is critical to fighting a fire and being able to disconnect all power supplies affecting fire-fighting operations. Note that to ensure reliability of the telecommunications network, batteries utilized in telecommunications power plants in central offices rarely have circuit breakers or an automatic disconnecting means in order to satisfy an FCC Best Practice designed to ensure the highest reliability. Such installations are not within the scope of SBC 401, and a disconnecting means is not required.

1207.4.2

Working clearances. Access and

working space shall be provided and maintained

about all electrical equipment to permit ready and

safe operation and maintenance of such equipment

in accordance with SBC 401 and the manufacturer′s

instructions.

❖ This section requires the proper spacing to reduce ignition of surrounding materials and to allow proper maintenance. This is consistent with Section 603.4.

1207.4.3

Fire-resistance-rated

separations. Rooms and other indoor areas

containing ESS shall be separated from other areas

of the building in accordance with Section 1207.7.4.

ESS shall be permitted to be in the same room with

the equipment they support.

❖ This section focuses on containment of the hazard to the location where the ESS is located, whether in an entire room or in a small space such as a closet. ESS being subject to thermal runaway could cause a fire that will continue to burn for an extended amount of time. Section 1207.7.4 requires 2-hour fire

-

barriers and 2-hour horizontal separations. In many cases, due to the impracticality of separating ESS from the equipment being supported, it is clarified that they may remain together in the same room.

1207.4.4

Seismic and structural design.

Stationary ESS shall comply with the seismic

design requirements in Chapter 16 of SBC 201, and

shall not exceed the floor loading limitation of the

building.

❖ Some of these installations are quite heavy, so much so that they can potentially affect the structural integrity of the building and the floor loading limitations need to be addressed. Also, to avoid additional failures during seismic events, the requirements of SBC 201 must be addressed. Assessment of rooftop installations on existing buildings must include whether structural fire resistance of the primary structural frame and bearing walls will remain in compliance with SBC 201 Table 601.

1207.4.5

Vehicle impact protection.

Where ESS are subject to impact by a motor

vehicle, including fork lifts, vehicle impact

protection shall be provided in accordance with

Section 312.

❖ This section provides the requirements for protecting the battery system from physical damage to avoid a malfunction of the systems and the hazards that could result. This applies to indoor or outdoor installations, depending on the potential impact risk from motor vehicles (see commentary, Section 312).

1207.4.6

Combustible

storage.

Combustible materials shall not be stored in ESS

rooms, areas or walk-in units. Combustible

materials in occupied work centers covered by

Section 1207.4.10 shall be stored at least 900 mm

from ESS cabinets.

❖ In the past, ESS were often independent of the rest of the building and its operations. In particular, the focus had only been on applications such as telecommunications buildings. However, there are many cases now where ESS are located in a variety of locations with other ongoing activities, such as data centers. This section requires a minimum separation from combustibles or prohibits the storage of combustible materials to reduce the risk of fire.

1207.4.7

Toxic and highly toxic gases. ESS

that have the potential to release toxic and highly

toxic gas during charging, discharging and normal

use conditions shall be provided with a hazardous

exhaust system in accordance with Section 502.8 of SBC 501.

❖ This section covers battery technologies (many yet to hit the market) that may potentially produce toxic gases during charging, discharging and normal use, and trigger Chapter 60 safety requirements. There is no intent to address batteries that produce toxic gases during fires or abnormal conditions. Hydrogen and oxygen produced by vented batteries are not considered toxic or highly toxic gases.

1207.4.8

Signage. Approved signs shall be

provided on or adjacent to all entry doors for ESS

rooms or areas and on enclosures of ESS cabinets

and walk-in units located outdoors, on rooftops or

in open parking garages. Signs designed to meet

both the requirements of this section and SBC 401

shall be permitted. The signage shall include the

following or equivalent:

1. “ENERGY STORAGE “BATTERY STORAGE “CAPACITOR ENERGY SYSTEM” or the equivalent.

SYSTEM,” SYSTEM,” STORAGE

2. The identification of the electrochemical ESS technology present.

3. “ENERGIZED CIRCUITS.”

ELECTRICAL

4. Where water-reactive electrochemical ESS are present, the signage shall include “APPLY NO WATER.”

5. Current contact information, including phone number, for personnel authorized to service the equipment and for fire mitigation personnel required by Section 1207.1.6.1.

Exception: Existing electrochemical ESS shall be permitted to include the signage required at the time they were installed.

❖ ESS are found in varying applications in today′s world. They are no longer simply for standby and emergency power, and technologies are varied and evolving quickly.

This section identifies the need for signage to identify the hazards. Items 1 and 2 require signage to denote where ESS are contained in a room and the type of technology present. Item 3 focuses on communicating through signage that energized electrical circuits are present.

Item 4 provides necessary information to occupants and emergency responders of any water-reactive properties and clearly

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

communicates through signage that water cannot be applied.

Item 5 addresses the need to provide contact information regarding personnel authorized to service the ESS and more importantly, fire mitigation personnel who are properly trained to manage the hazards associated with the ESS. See Section 1207.1.6.1.

The exception addresses currently installed systems that were not required to have the prescribed signage at the time of installation, which often will be standalone lead-acid battery systems. These types of installations have had a good safety record and typically do not create exposure hazards based on their chemistries and the way they are used and regulated.

1207.4.9

Security of installations. Rooms,

areas and walk-in units in which electrochemical

ESS are located shall be secured against

unauthorized entry and safeguarded in an approved

manner. Security barriers, fences, landscaping and

other enclosures shall not inhibit the required air

flow to or exhaust from the electrochemical ESS

and its components.

❖ This section is general to all ESS installation types, whether indoors or outdoors. Properly securing such installations reduces the risk of arson or vandalism, which could lead to a failure of such systems. This is more of a concern with outdoor installations or walk-in units. It is important that the security features do not negatively impact the ventilation requirements for the installation. Facilities with a secured perimeter and controlled access to the site would qualify, and additional securing of an ESS installation on such a site would not be required.

1207.4.10 Occupied work centers. Electrochemical ESS located in rooms or areas occupied by personnel not directly involved with maintenance, service and testing of the systems shall comply with the following:

1. Electrochemical ESS located in occupied work centers shall be housed in locked noncombustible cabinets or other enclosures to prevent access by unauthorized personnel.

2. Where electrochemical ESS are contained in cabinets in occupied work centers, the cabinets shall be located within 3 m of the equipment that they support.

3. Cabinets shall include signage complying with Section 1207.4.8.

❖ In cases where the ESS is located where

building occupants could be present, some level of protection is necessary.
This section first requires the ESS to be secured. Similar to Section 1207.4.9, this reduces a possible failure of the system due to vandalism or arson.
Placing battery system cabinets within 3 m of the equipment served by the system will limit the number of devices that can be powered by the system due to the voltage drop associated with longer or multiple conductor runs. This section also enhances the safety of personnel who may have to secure and work on equipment served by the battery system by placing the secondary power supply near enough to the powered equipment to ensure line-of-sight supervision of the secured battery system during equipment repair or maintenance.
This section calls for the identification of the specific hazards associated with the ESS contained in each cabinet. ESS technologies vary and with that, the hazard varies. This information will also provide the capacity of each system, which is essential for the safety of the occupants and first responders.
1207.4.11 Open rack installations. Where electrochemical ESS are installed in a separate equipment room and only authorized personnel have access to the room, they shall be permitted to be installed on an open rack for ease of maintenance.
❖ This section essentially allows ESS to be on open racks where access to the room is restricted. The purpose is to facilitate maintenance, which could have the effect of better safety performance. Essentially, the ESS utilizes the room as a means of separation in place of cabinet enclosures.
1207.4.12 Walk-in units. Walk-in units shall be entered only for inspection, maintenance and repair of ESS units and ancillary equipment, and shall not be occupied for other purposes.
❖ This section clarifies the intent of walk-in units. Such units are not intended for regular occupancy and are only to be entered for the purposes of maintenance and repairs. Those entering are trained to do so. Although occupancy is restricted, ESS walk-in units are defined as buildings, and all requirements applicable to buildings apply, including fire suppression protection.
1207.5 Electrochemical ESS protection. The protection of electrochemical ESS shall be in accordance with Sections 1207.5.1 through

1207.5.8 where required by Sections 1207.7 through 1207.10.

❖ This section generally sets out the specific protection required for the ESS itself. Section 1207.4 focuses on the elements surrounding such ESS but not the specific protection of the equipment. The triggers for these protection features vary based on the technology used and the type of installation addressed in Sections 1207.7 through 1207.10 (indoor installations, outdoor installations and special installations such as rooftop installations and mobile ESS equipment).

This table sets out the MAQ of each technology that can be found in a single installation within rooms, areas and walk-in units. This serves as a trigger to the application of Section 1207.1.5 and large-scale fire tests, which will provide important data necessary for the determination of effective protection features (see Section 1207.5.2, Item 1). Certain technologies are unlimited in capacity based on their historically good safety record. This does not exempt such technologies from protection; it is simply the size of the installation that is not limited.

1207.5.1

Size

and

separation.

Electrochemical ESS shall be segregated into

groups not exceeding 50 kWh (180 megajoules).

Each group shall be separated a minimum of 914

mm from other groups and from walls in the storage

room or area. The storage arrangements shall

comply with Chapter 10.

Exceptions:

1. Lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery systems in facilities under the exclusive control of communications utilities and operating at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC in accordance with NFPA 76.

2. The Fire official is authorized to approve larger capacities or smaller separation distances based on large-scale fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5.

❖ In an effort to reduce the possible size and intensity of a fire or explosion, ESS units are limited in capacity and must be separated into certain capacity groups. Each group must have a fire break of essentially 900 mm from other groups and walls. Exception1 acknowledges the safety record of lead-acid and nickelcadmium batteries and does not require the 50 kWh and 900 mm separation. Exception 2 provides flexibility to the base capacity of the ESS group if testing determines that a fire would be contained to the room as separated. It should also be noted that the types of

installation that are addressed in Sections 1207.7 through 1207.10 will affect whether these size and separation requirements apply. For example, Table 1207.8 for remote outdoor installations would not require compliance with this section.

1207.5.2

Maximum allowable quantities.

Fire areas within rooms, areas and walk-in units

containing electrochemical ESS shall not exceed

the maximum allowable quantities in Table 1207.5.

Exceptions:

1. Where approved by the Fire official, rooms, areas and walk-in units containing electrochemical ESS that exceed the amounts in Table 1207.5 shall be permitted based on a hazardous mitigation analysis in accordance with Section 1207.1.4 and largescale fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5.

2. Lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery systems installed in facilities under the exclusive control of communications utilities, and operating at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC in accordance with NFPA 76.

3. Dedicated-use buildings in compliance with Section 1207.7.1.

❖ Scientific research and large-scale fire and fault condition testing are not available to justify allowing unlimited quantities of energy in an ESS to be provided in a single fire area. By referencing Table 1207.5, this section establishes a maximum 600 kWh MAQ for each fire area and 200 kWh for certain technologies for which less information is available. The MAQ of 600 kWh is equivalent to 1,250 storage batteries, each rated 12 V, 40 Ah. In addition, capacitors and other electrochemical ESS are limited to 20 kWh.

There are several exceptions to these requirements. Exception 1 allows such installations to be reviewed through a hazards mitigation analysis and large-scale fire testing results to justify larger amounts of ESS.

Exception 2 recognizes the safety record of leadacid and nickel-cadmium battery systems under the control of communications utilities.

Exception 3 recognizes the minimal life safety hazard associated with dedicated-use buildings that comply with Section 1207.7.1.

1207.5.2.1 Mixed electrochemical energy systems. Where rooms, areas and walk-in units contain different types of electrochemical energy technologies, the total aggregate quantities of the

-

systems shall be determined based on the sum of percentages of each technology-type quantity divided by the maximum allowable quantity of each technology type. The sum of the percentages shall not exceed 100 percent of the maximum allowable quantity.

❖ Because of the changing technologies of ESS, it is becoming more common to have multiple technologies in a single building. This section addresses how to determine the MAQ where multiple ESS are used. Note that due to the safety record of lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery systems, the MAQ are unlimited. If, for example, there are flow batteries and sodium batteries in the same fire area, the allowable installed quantity permitted under this section of the code is determined on a percentage basis as follows:

Given:

450 kWh flow battery system

75 kWh other battery system

450 kWh flow battery system = 450 kWh/600 kWh = 0.75 × 100 = 75%

75 kWh other battery technology system = 75 kWh/200 kWh = 0.375 × 100 = 37.5%

75% (flow) + 37.5% (other) = 112.5% of MAQ; Therefore, hazard mitigation analysis would be required for other than dedicated-use buildings.

1207.5.3

Elevation. Electrochemical ESS

shall not be located in the following areas:

1. Where the floor is located more than 23 m above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access.

2. Where the floor is located below the lowest level of exit discharge.

Exceptions:

1. Lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery systems less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC installed in facilities under the exclusive control of communications utilities in accordance with NFPA 76.

2. Where approved, installations shall be permitted in underground vaults complying with SBC 401, Article 450, Part III.

3. Where approved by the Fire official, installations shall be permitted on higher and lower floors.

❖ In terms of location, the main concern is access for fire fighting and potential intensity of fires

from certain ESS such as lithium ion. ESS are becoming more common in high-rise buildings and without this section, such installations can make it much more difficult for the fire department to fight a fire. This section does not prohibit installations in high-rise buildings but instead limits the location of such systems to the low-rise portion of the building. A key component for fire attack is the amount of available water, and 23 m was chosen based on a fire department ′s ability to use pre-piped elevating tower ladders or ladders to bring a larger water supply to upper floors. Likewise, underground buildings also are addressed. Fires involving ESS can generate tremendous amounts of smoke and heat, which fire fighters would have to climb down into for floors lower than the level of exit discharge. These restrictions were specifically added based on concerns voiced by the operations side of the fire service.

Exception 1 is provided for lead-acid and nickel-cadmium storage battery systems, as they have a good safety record and further restrictions beyond that provided in previous editions of this code are not necessary.

Exception 2 allows installations in underground vaults that comply with SBC 401 that essentially provides 3 hours of fire-resistancerated construction.

Exception 3 gives some flexibility to unique situations where the risks to such installations are properly mitigated and such installations are specifically approved. The amount of water available for fire fighting to floors over 23 m above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access would be a consideration for installations on higher floors. The ability to control and exhaust heat and smoke from lower floors would be a consideration for allowing installation on lower floors.

1207.5.4

Fire detection. An approved

automatic smoke detection system or radiant

energy-sensing fire detection system complying

with Section 907.2 shall be installed in rooms,

indoor areas and walk-in units containing

electrochemical ESS. An approved radiant energy-

sensing fire detection system shall be installed to

protect open parking garage and rooftop

installations. Alarm signals from detection systems

shall be transmitted to a central station, proprietary

or remote station service in accordance with NFPA

72, or where approved to a constantly attended

location.

❖ Many ESS fires are likely to be slow growing and smoldering; therefore, a smoke detection

system or radiant energy-sensing fire detection system is required for early detection and warning in accordance with Section 907.2 where the system requires application of Section 1207 (i.e., exceeds the threshold quantities in Table 1207.1.1). All types of installations addressed by Sections 1207.7 through 1207.10 require fire detection. For example, where an ESS using lithium-ion batteries exceeds 20 kWh, a smoke detection or radiant energy-sensing system must be provided. The goal is early warning of an event that the fire service needs to respond to. For radiant-energy detection, that would equate to thermal imaging or multi-spectrum infrared flame detectors, both of which are recognized by NFPA 72.

1207.5.4.1 System status. Where required by the Fire official, visible annunciation shall be provided on cabinet exteriors or in other approved locations to indicate that potentially hazardous conditions associated with the ESS exist.

❖ This section provides authority to the Fire official to require visible notification in situations where it is deemed necessary to alert emergency responders directly of the conditions at the location of the hazard. A constantly attended location may not be at the equipment itself.

Fire suppression systems. Rooms

and areas within buildings and walk-in units

containing electrochemical ESS shall be protected

by an automatic fire suppression system designed

and installed in accordance with one of the

following:

1. An automatic sprinkler system designed and installed in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1 with a minimum density of 1.15 L/min based on the fire area or 230 m2 design area, whichever is smaller.

2. Where approved, an automatic sprinkler system designed and installed in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1 with a sprinkler hazard classification based on large-scale fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5.

3. The following alternative automatic fireextinguishing systems designed and installed in accordance with Section 904, provided that the installation is approved by the Fire official based on large-scale fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5:

3.2. NFPA 15, Standard for Water Spray Fixed Systems for Fire Protection.
3.3. NFPA 750, Standard on Water Mist Fire Protection Systems.
3.4. NFPA 2001, Standard on Clean Agent Fire-Extinguishing Systems.
3.5. 3.5.NFPA 2010, Standard for Fixed Aerosol Fire-Extinguishing Systems.
Exception: Fire suppression systems for lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery systems at facilities under the exclusive control of communications utilities that operate at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC shall be provided where required by NFPA 76.
❖ Suppression systems are required for most types of ESS as addressed in Sections 1207.7 through 1207.10 with the exception of rooftop and open parking garage installations open to the sky or open parking garages where fullscale testing demonstrates that such protection is not required (see Section 1207.9.4).
Section 1207.5.5.1 addresses battery systems that contain water-reactive materials where water cannot be used.
This section provides three options. Item 1 provides a prescriptive requirement for the use of automatic sprinkler systems with specific design criteria. Item 2 allows a differentiation from this criterion where demonstrated to perform during a large-scale fire test. Likewise, Item 3 allows alternative automatic suppression systems where performance is demonstrated through a large-scale fire test similar to that in Item 2. Essentially, Items 2 and 3 must specifically pass UL 9540A.
The telecommunications industry has continually stressed the need for the continuity of telephone service, and the ability to maintain this service is of prime importance. This service is a vital link between the community and the various life safety services, including fire, police and emergency medical services. The integrity of this communications service can be jeopardized not only by fire, but also by water, from whatever the source. The telecommunications industry utilizes other means to achieve an exceptional record of fire safety and reliability. The exception applies only to those spaces or areas that are used exclusively for telecommunications equipment. Historically, those spaces have a low incidence of fire events. Fires in telecommunications equipment are difficult to start and, if started, grow slowly, thus permitting early detection. Such fires are typically of the smoldering type,

do not spread beyond the immediate area and generally self-extinguish.

1207.5.5.1 Water-reactive

systems.

Electrochemical ESS that utilize water-reactive

materials shall be protected by an approved

alternative automatic fire-extinguishing system in

accordance with Section 904, where the installation

It should be noted that some battery systems such as lithium ion might be water reactive but can, in some cases, be suppressed with water effectively. This section requires that the system be based on full-scale fire and fault condition testing. Item 3 of Section 1207.5.5 essentially allows the same concept based on largescale testing.

Maximum enclosure size.

Outdoor walk-in units housing ESS shall not exceed

16 m × 2.4 m × 2.85 m) high, not including bolt-on

HVAC and related equipment, as approved.

Outdoor walk-in units exceeding these limitations

Vegetation control. Areas within

3 m on each side of outdoor ESS shall be cleared of

combustible vegetation and other combustible

growth. Single specimens of trees, shrubbery or

❖ This provision reduces exposure from exterior fires on the ESS. Some ground cover is acceptable as long as such ground cover does not create a means of readily transmitting fire. Essentially, well-manicured grass poses little hazard; however, it must be maintained to reduce the risk of possible fire spread to the ESS.

Means of egress separation. ESS

located outdoors and in open parking garages shall

be separated from any means of egress as required

by the Fire official to ensure safe egress under fire

conditions, but in no case less than 3 m.

Exception: The Fire official is authorized to approve a reduced separation distance if large-scale fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5 is provided that shows that a fire involving the ESS will not adversely impact occupant egress.

The exception allows a lesser separation where large-scale testing demonstrates that a hazard does not exist. This provides flexibility for new technologies or situations where a 3 m separation is not possible.
1207.6 Electrochemical ESS technology-specific protection. Electrochemical ESS installations shall comply with the requirements of this section in accordance with the applicable requirements of Table 1207.6.
❖ This section simply introduces Table 1207.6, which provides technology-specific protection. These features include exhaust ventilation, explosion control, safety caps, spill control and neutralization, and thermal runaway. These features are based on each specific technology. For instance, lead-acid batteries contain liquid and will need spill control and neutralization whereas lithium-ion batteries do not as they do not have a liquid to spill. However, both technologies are subject to thermal runaway and need proper controls to address this in accordance with Section 1207.6.5.
Note a relieves communications utilities regulated by NFPA 76 from having to provide explosion control for lead-acid and nickelcadmium batteries given the capacity of the systems and the safety record of such facilities.
Note b states that unless proper documentation is provided for other and perhaps newer technologies, all of these features are required. The default is the more restrictive application of the requirements without further information about the performance of these ESS technologies.
Note c addresses the fact that spill control and neutralization for lead-acid and nickelcadmium batteries is required only where such batteries are vented and have the ability to create a spill.
Note d acknowledges the fact that vented leadacid batteries are not likely to go into thermal runaway.
Note e allows the use of a battery management system that was tested with the ESS to UL 1973 for management of thermal runaway
