-

hazards.

See the commentary to Section 1207.6.

1207.6.1

Exhaust ventilation. Where

required by Table 1207.6 or elsewhere in this code,

exhaust ventilation of rooms, areas and walk-in

units containing electrochemical ESS shall be

provided in accordance with SBC 501 and Section

1207.6.1.1 or 1207.6.1.2.

❖ This section addresses the necessary ventilation requirements based on either the conclusion of the hazard mitigation analysis or the specific battery technology requirements. Some batteries need more ventilation than others.

As with any other building areas, ventilation of ESS areas must comply with the requirements of SBC 501. In many cases, basic ventilation based on human comfort will be sufficient to adequately ventilate battery spaces. The main concern with many types of batteries is the buildup of hydrogen within an enclosed space. This is not the case for lithium-ion batteries, but it is a concern for aqueous batteries such as lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery types, which contain water. Hydrogen has a wide flammability range and is the lightest element on the Periodic Table of Elements, having an atomic number of 1. The gas mixture is potentially explosive when the amount of hydrogen in the air exceeds 4 percent by volume. There are two options provided for ventilation addressed in Section 1207.6.1.1 or 1207.6.1.2. Sections 1207.6.1.2.1 through 1207.6.1.2.3 apply to either approach taken for ventilation. Section 1207.6.1.2.4 is more specific to the method of ventilation addressed in Section 1207.6.1.2.

1207.6.1.1 Ventilation based on LFL. The exhaust ventilation system shall be designed to limit the maximum concentration of flammable gas to 25 percent of the lower flammable limit (LFL) of the total volume of the room, area or walk-in unit during the worst-case event of simultaneous charging of batteries at the maximum charge rate, in accordance with nationally recognized standards.

❖ This section provides a performance-based approach by requiring a ventilation system designed to limit the maximum concentration of flammable gas to 25 percent of the LFL of the total volume of the room, area or walk-in unit with the worst-case event of charging at the maximum charging rate.

1207.6.1.2 Ventilation based on exhaust
rate. Mechanical exhaust ventilation shall be provided at a rate of not less than 5.1 L/sec/m2 of

floor area of the room, area or walk-in unit. The ventilation shall be either continuous or shall be activated by a gas detection system in accordance with Section 1207.6.1.2.4.

❖ This section simply prescribes a ventilation rate of not less than 0.0051 m3/(s × m2) of room area. This is an extremely conservative ventilation rate. This section does not require continuous ventilation where ventilation is activated by a gas detection system.

1207.6.1.2.1 Standby power. Mechanical exhaust ventilation shall be provided with a minimum of 2 hours of standby power in accordance with Section 1203.2.5.

❖ Regardless of the approach used for ventilation, mechanical ventilation is required to be provided with 2 hours of standby power. This is very important with technologies subject to thermal runaway that can be continuously generating more flammable gases.

1207.6.1.2.2 Installation

instructions.

Required mechanical exhaust ventilation systems

shall be installed in accordance with the

manufacturer′s installation instructions and SBC

501.

❖ This section is merely ensuring that ventilation systems are installed as intended by the manufacturer and as required by SBC 501 for optimal performance.

1207.6.1.2.3 Supervision. Required mechanical exhaust ventilation systems shall be supervised by an approved central station, proprietary or remote station service in accordance with NFPA 72, or shall initiate an audible and visible signal at an approved constantly attended on-site location.

❖ Where mechanical ventilation is utilized to keep the concentration of flammable gases, such as hydrogen, from reaching a flammable or explosive limit, some method of ensuring that the ventilation system is operational is necessary. This section provides two options, which include supervision or an audible and visual alarm at an approved location. A security desk that is operated 24 hours a day would be a reasonable location to meet the “constantly attended” criterion.

It should be noted that according to the IEEE 1635, ventilation rates can be very low. Hydrogen is extremely small and will pass through walls and ceilings. Maintaining a concentration of hydrogen in building construction is difficult. One example of a facility exploding due to hydrogen from batteries is the Rancho Cordova, California,

SBC 801-CC-2024

1029

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

data center explosion. The facility was unoccupied and not air conditioned, with batteries on charge with no load. It took months for the hydrogen to build to explosive levels. When treated properly, aqueous batteries do not explode. In many cases, the normal ventilation for human comfort in accordance with SBC 501 is more than sufficient; however, supervision is required for the ventilation system.

1207.6.1.2.4 Gas detection system. Where required by Section 1207.6.1.2, rooms, areas and walk-in units containing ESS shall be protected by an approved continuous gas detection system that complies with Section 916 and with the following:

1. The gas detection system shall be designed to activate the mechanical ventilation system when the level of flammable gas in the room, area or walk-in unit exceeds 25 percent of the LFL.

2. The mechanical ventilation system shall remain on until the flammable gas detected is less than 25 percent of the LFL.

3. The gas detection system shall be provided with a minimum of 2 hours of standby power in accordance with Section 1203.2.5.

4. Failure of the gas detection system shall annunciate a trouble signal at an approved central station, proprietary or remote station service in accordance with NFPA 72, or shall initiate an audible and visible trouble signal at an approved constantly attended on-site location.

❖ This section states the actions that must occur once the gas detection system activates. Since gas detection is an alternative to continuously running ventilation (see Section 1207.6.1.2), when the gas detection system detects that 25 percent of the LFL has been exceeded, the ventilation system must be activated. Once the flammable gas is below 25 percent, the mechanical ventilation is no longer necessary. Standby power is required for 2 hours for such systems in case an event occurs during a power outage. That is consistent with the standby power required by Section 1207.6.1.2.1. Finally, another layer of protection is required by Item 4, which is detection of a failure of the gas detection system.

1207.6.2

Spill control and neutralization.

Where required by Table 1207.6 or elsewhere in

this code, areas containing freeflowing liquid

electrolyte or hazardous materials shall be provided

with spill control and neutralization in accordance with this section.
❖ This section is specific to free-flowing liquid electrolyte or other free-flowing liquid hazardous materials. Spill control and neutralization are required by Table 1207.6 for most types of battery technologies. Lithiumion and nonvented lead-acid, nickel-cadmium and nickel metal hydride batteries would not require this protection since there is little to no risk of liquid spills. Note that documentation can be provided that demonstrates spill control and neutralization are not necessary for ESS technologies not specifically called out in Table 1207.6.
Batteries that contain a free-flowing liquid electrolyte pose the same containment problems as any other corrosive liquid hazardous materials, but the containment and neutralization provisions in this section are performance based and neither specifically require spill control in the form of containment nor a specific method of neutralization. The quantity of neutralization material required to be available would be greater for less-viscous electrolytes because of their mobility and the rapidity with which they can spread and the potential scope of the spread.
1207.6.2.1 Spill control. Spill control shall be provided to prevent the flow of liquid electrolyte or hazardous materials to adjoining rooms or areas. The method shall be capable of containing a spill from the single largest battery or vessel.
❖ This section simply sets the performance criteria for spill control: stopping the flow from entering adjoining rooms or areas and basing that on the largest single battery.
1207.6.2.2 Neutralization. An approved method that is capable of neutralizing spilled liquid electrolyte from the largest battery or vessel to a pH between 5.0 and 9.0 shall be provided.
❖ In addition to spill control where a battery releases liquid electrolyte, the liquid must be neutralized. This requirement is specifically performance based to be flexible for different technologies.
1207.6.2.3 Communications utilities. The requirements of Section 1207.6.2 shall apply only when the aggregate capacity of multiple vessels exceeds 3785 L for leadacid and nickel-cadmium battery systems operating at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC that are located at facilities under the exclusive control of communications utilities, and those facilities comply with NFPA 76 in addition to applicable requirements of this code.

❖ This section recognizes the good safety record and level of proper enforcement through compliance with NFPA 76 of these technologies in this particular application.

1207.6.3

Explosion control. Where

required by Table 1207.6 or elsewhere in this code,

explosion control complying with Section 911 shall

be provided for rooms, areas or walk-in units

containing electrochemical ESS technologies.

Exceptions:

1. Where approved, explosion control is permitted to be waived by the Fire official based on largescale fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5 that demonstrates that flammable gases are not liberated from electrochemical ESS cells or modules where tested in accordance with UL 9540A.

2. Where approved, explosion control is permitted to be waived by the Fire official based on documentation provided in accordance with Section 104.7 that demonstrates that the electrochemical ESS technology to be used does not have the potential to release flammable gas concentrations in excess of 25 percent of the LFL anywhere in the room, area, walk-in unit or structure under thermal runaway or other fault conditions.

❖ This section addresses a potentially significant hazard. Lithium-ion battery systems and other electrochemical ESS technologies have the potential to rapidly build up potentially explosive atmospheres in the battery or electrochemical ESS room or enclosure under thermal runaway and other conditions, which could result in a catastrophic fire and/or explosion. To protect against these hazards, explosion control in accordance with Section 911 is required for certain battery technologies. Note that Table 1207.6 requires explosion control for all technologies except flow control and lead-acid, nickel-cadmium and nickel metal hydride batteries. The exemption for lead-acid, nickel-cadmium and nickel metal hydride relates to the safety record of these applications specific to communications utilities.

Exception 1 allows the elimination of explosion control where a large-scale fire test demonstrates that flammable bases are not liberated from the ESS. Exception 2 does not specifically call for a large-scale test, but technical justification is required to demonstrate that a dangerous concentration of flammable gases is not released.

1207.6.4

Safety caps. Where required by

Table 1207.6 or elsewhere in this code, vented

batteries and other ESS shall be provided with

flame-arresting safety caps.

❖ The types of batteries regulated by this section vent hydrogen and oxygen to the atmosphere. There are two types of vented batteries: nonrecombinant and recombinant. Essentially, a nonrecombinant battery is a storage battery in which, under conditions of normal use, hydrogen and oxygen gases created by electrolysis are vented into the air outside of the battery. A recombinant battery is a storage battery in which, under conditions of normal use, most of the hydrogen and oxygen gases created by electrolysis are converted back into water inside the battery instead of venting into the air.

Another term used for nonrecombinant batteries is “flooded” or “vented.” Other terms used to denote recombinant batteries are “valve regulated” or “valveregulated lead-acid (VRLA).” Flooded batteries vent regularly whereas valve-regulated batteries vent when the internal pressure exceeds the design pressure of their self-resealing vents, which are an integral part of their design. In case of either flooded or VRLA cells, flame-arresting vents are needed to prevent a static spark (or other flame source) outside the battery from propagating to the interior of the battery where oxygen or hydrogen may have accumulated during normal operation of the battery.

Essentially, all ESS technologies except lithium-ion and flow batteries require safety caps. Other technologies not named in Table 1207.6 would need to provide documentation demonstrating that safety caps are not necessary.

1207.6.5

Thermal runaway. Where

required by Table 1207.6 or elsewhere in this code,

batteries and other ESS shall be provided with a

listed device or other approved method to prevent,

detect and minimize the impact of thermal runaway.

❖ This requirement is applicable only where required by the battery-specific requirements of Table 1207.6. Under certain extreme conditions of high ambient temperature or charging rate, or due to internal partial shorting in cells, VRLA batteries may experience a phenomenon known as “thermal runaway.” This is a condition that occurs in a battery (especially valve-regulated types) where charging energy results in heat generation within the battery greater than the heat dissipated, causing an uncontrolled rise in battery temperature. Under these conditions, a

SBC 801-CC-2024

1031

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

battery may generate excessive heat that can cause failure through cell dryout or, in rare cases, rupture or melting of the battery.
Lithium-ion technologies are also subject to thermal runaway. Most often, damage or a short circuit can lead to runaway. The chemicals that are released during thermal runaway can be toxic and in extreme cases, thermal runaway can cause electrical fires and/ or batteries to explode. The ambient air temperature in the battery environment must also be properly maintained. Controlling these factors reduces the potential for thermal runaway.
This section requires either a listed device that can control thermal runaway or other approved methods. There are a variety of ESS types, so a specific method cannot be prescribed to address this hazard. The only battery technology not required to address thermal runaway are flow batteries and vented leadacid batteries. Lithium-ion batteries are permitted to address thermal runaway through a battery management system in accordance with UL 1973.
1207.7 Indoor installations. Indoor ESS installations shall be in accordance with Sections 1207.7.1 through 1207.7.4.
❖ Section 1207.7 and Table 1207.7 cover indoor locations and identify two types of indoor installations, dedicated-use installations (typical of utility grid-related facilities) and nondedicated-use installations (typical of ESS in mixed-use buildings or incidental-use areas of occupancies). Protection for each installation is commensurate
Table 1207.7 maps out which safety requirements are applicable to dedicated-use buildings and nondedicated- use buildings. The requirements focus on location and elevation of installations, whether fire suppression is required, how such installations shall be separated (fire resistance and distance), smoke and fire detection requirements and how the technology-specific requirements apply. The main differences between the two types of installations are that dedicated-use buildings do not have MAQs and have no specific exposure concerns for dwelling and sleeping units. They are nonoccupied so the life safety risk is much lower. In addition, Note c allows omission of fire suppression if the dedicated-use building is more than 30 m from occupied buildings since it poses a low life safety hazard. Alarm signals are not required to be transmitted if local alarm annunciation is provided along with constantly present, trained

personnel. Dedicated-use buildings, overall, offer less risk to human life as such buildings are occupied only by those running the facility.

1207.7.1

Dedicated-use buildings. For the

purpose of Table 1207.7, dedicated-use ESS

buildings shall be classified as Group F-1

occupancies and comply with all the following:

1. The building shall only be used for ESS, electrical energy generation and other electrical grid-related operations.

2. Occupants in the rooms and areas containing ESS are limited to personnel that operate, maintain, service, test and repair the ESS and other energy systems.

3. No other occupancy types shall be permitted in the building.

4. Administrative and support personnel shall be permitted in areas within the buildings that do not contain ESS, provided that:

4.1. The areas do not occupy more than 10 percent of the building area of the story in which they are located.

4.2. A means of egress is provided from the incidental use areas to the public way that does not require occupants to traverse through areas containing ESS or other energy system equipment.

❖ Such facilities focus only on ESS, electrical energy generation or other electrical gridrelated operations. The only people that may be present are those that operate, maintain, service test or repair such facilities. This section requires that the occupancy classification is F-1. This is also denoted in SBC 201 Section 306.2. Such facilities cannot contain other occupancies. Due to the restrictive use of such buildings, more flexibility is given to quantities of ESS and in certain locations, as discussed in Table 1207.7, omission of fire suppression systems or smoke or fire detection.

1207.7.2

Nondedicated-use buildings. For

the purpose of Table 1207.7, nondedicated-use

buildings include all buildings that contain ESS and

do not comply with Section 1207.7.1 dedicated-use

building requirements.

❖ Nondedicated-use buildings are simply any buildings or facilities that do not comply with Section 1207.7.1. Once other occupancies and associated occupants are introduced, the risk to life safety increases.

1207.7.3

Dwelling units and sleeping

units. ESS shall not be installed in sleeping units or

in habitable spaces of dwelling units.

❖ This section simply prohibits ESS within dwelling units and sleeping units due to the various hazards associated with ESS.

1207.7.4

Fire-resistance-rated

separations. Rooms and areas containing ESS shall

include fire-resistance-rated separations as follows:

1. In dedicated-use buildings, rooms and areas containing ESS shall be separated from areas in which administrative and support personnel are located.

2. In nondedicated-use buildings, rooms and areas containing ESS shall be separated from other areas in the building.

3. Separation shall be provided by 2-hour fire barriers constructed in accordance with Section 707 of SBC 201 and 2-hour horizontal assemblies constructed in accordance with Section 711 of SBC 201, as appropriate.

❖ Essentially, whether dedicated use or nondedicated use, certain aspects of such buildings must be separated by 2-hour fire barriers or 2-hour horizontal separations. The focus is on separating occupied areas from the ESS directly.

1207.8 Outdoor installations. Outdoor installations shall be in accordance with Sections 1207.8.1 through 1207.8.3. Exterior wall installations for individual ESS units not exceeding 20 kWh shall be in accordance with Section 1207.8.4.

❖ This section covers two types of outdoor installations, remote outdoor installations (more than 30 m from exposures) and installations near exposures (30 m or less, which is more typical of an urban environment). Also note that ESS units are permitted to be installed on exterior walls where they do not exceed 20 kWh. Compliance with Section 1207.8.4 is required. Section 1207.8.4 limits the capacity of such systems and requires each ESS unit to be separated by 900 mm to reduce possible fire exposure to an adjacent system.

Table 1207.8 provides the triggers for compliance for outdoor ESS installations. Outdoor installations are classified as either a remote installation or an installation located near exposures. The differentiating aspect is location. Where an installation is more than 30

m from buildings, lot lines, public ways, stored combustible materials, hazardous materials, high-piled stock and other exposures, it can be treated as remote. All other installations would be dealt with as installations near exposures and are more likely in urban or suburban locations.

Remote installations are excused from some safety features due to the lower impact on life safety. There is no limit on quantities; however, there is a maximum enclosure size. The intent of the maximum enclosure size is to differentiate between indoor storage (building) versus outdoor installation. Section 1207.5.6 corresponds to the largest ISO-type containers used for these installations. Size and separation are also not limited, which means there is no limit on capacity of each ESS or separation from the walls or other ESS.

Notes a and b simply clarify which ESS installations are considered remote and which are not. Note c gives the authority to the Fire official to omit the requirement for fire suppression. This is especially important for very remote installations where the risk is low and the water supply availability or infrastructure to support such systems is nonexistent. Finally, Note d allows an exception to the spacing from the wall for nonremote installations. The maximum size of 50kWh and the 900 mm separation from other groups still applies. As discussed, remote installations have no size or separation limitations for ESS groups.

1207.8.1

Remote outdoor installations.

For the purpose of Table 1207.8, remote outdoor

installations include ESS located more than 30 m

from buildings, lot lines, public ways, stored

combustible materials, hazardous materials, high-

piled stock and other exposure hazards.

❖ This section establishes what can be considered a remote installation. As discussed in the commentary to Table 1207.8, such installations are not required to address quantities or size and separation requirements. However, many other features such as smoke and automatic fire detection are still required.

1207.8.2

Installations near exposures. For

the purpose of Table 1207.8, installations near

exposures include all outdoor ESS installations that

do not comply with Section 1207.8.1 remote

outdoor location requirements.

❖ Essentially, any outdoor installations that cannot be treated as remote must comply with the criteria for those installations 30 m or closer to exposures.

1207.8.3

Clearance to exposures. ESS

located outdoors shall be separated by a minimum

of 3 m from the following exposures:

1. Lot lines.

2. Public ways.

3. Buildings.

4. Stored combustible materials.

5. Hazardous materials.

6. High-piled stock.

7. Other exposure hazards.

Exceptions:

1. Clearances are permitted to be reduced to 900 mm where a 1-hour free-standing fire barrier suitable for exterior use and extending 1.5 m above and 1.5 m beyond the physical boundary of the ESS installation is provided to protect the exposure.

2. Clearances to buildings are permitted to be reduced to 900 mm where noncombustible exterior walls with no openings or combustible overhangs are provided on the wall adjacent to the ESS and the fireresistance rating of the exterior wall is a minimum of 2 hours.

3. Clearances to buildings are permitted to be reduced to 900 mm where a weatherproof enclosure constructed of noncombustible materials is provided over the ESS, and it has been demonstrated that a fire within the enclosure will not ignite combustible materials outside the enclosure based on large-scale fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5.

❖ This section primarily applies to installations that are not remote since such exposures are not permitted for remote installations. This section deals with the separation from exposure hazards with the intent of reducing the risk of an ESS failure and any resulting fire or explosion. Likewise, a failure of the ESS should not pose a hazard to surrounding exposures. There are three exceptions that reduce the separation to 900 mm. These options become critical in densely populated areas. The first two focus on the use of fireresistancerated construction to separate the ESS from exposures. The distance can be reduced to 900 mm for both. Exception 1 allows a 1-hour rating where a standalone barrier is used. Exception 2 allows the use of building construction with no openings but requires a 2-

hour rating. Exception 3 reduces the separation to 900 mm where an ESS installation is protected by a weatherproof enclosure of noncombustible construction that demonstrates during a large-scale fire test that the fire will not spread beyond the enclosure.

1207.8.4

Exterior wall installations. ESS

shall be permitted to be installed outdoors on

exterior walls of buildings when all of the following

conditions are met:

1. The maximum energy capacity of individual ESS units shall not exceed 20 kWh.

2. The ESS shall comply with applicable requirements in Section 1207.

3. The ESS shall be installed in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions and their listing.

4. Individual ESS units shall be separated from each other by at least 900 mm.

5. The ESS shall be separated from doors, windows, operable openings into buildings or HVAC inlets by at least 1.5 m.

Exception: Where approved, smaller separation distances in Items 4 and 5 shall be permitted based on large-scale fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5.

❖ Exterior wall installations are also considered outdoor installations. They are permitted but only if they comply with the criteria set out in this section. In addition to general compliance with Section 1207 and the use of properly installed and listed equipment, these ESS units are limited to 20 kWh and must be separated from one another by 900 mm. In terms of exposures to the building they are located near, such installations need to be at least 1.5 m from openings, including HVAC inlets. The exception provides the flexibility to conduct large-scale fire testing to allow smaller separation distances.

and not enclosed by building construction shall comply with the following:

1. Stairway access to the roof for emergency response and fire department personnel shall be provided either through a bulkhead from the interior of the building or a stairway on the exterior of the building.

2. Service walkways at least 1.5 m in width shall be provided for service and emergency personnel from the point of access to the roof to the system.

3. ESS and associated equipment shall be located from the edge of the roof a distance equal to at least the height of the system, equipment or component but not less than 1.5 m.

4. The roofing materials under and within 1.5 m horizontally from an ESS or associated equipment shall be noncombustible or shall have a Class A rating when tested in accordance with ASTM E108 or UL 790.

5. A Class I standpipe outlet shall be installed at an approved location on the roof level of the building or in the stairway bulkhead at the top level.

6. The ESS shall be the minimum of 3 m from the fire service access point on the rooftop.

❖ This section is specific to ESS installations on roofs. The requirement focuses on the needs of the fire department . The fire department may be in a position of responding to a fire associated with an ESS installation. Having appropriate features that will assist in this response is necessary. Proper stairway access and the ability to move about the roof is necessary. For further safety, such installations must be moved away from the edge of the roof. This will help fire fighters attack from more angles, reduce the risk of falls and decrease the possibility of debris falling from the roof. The access point needs to be of sufficient distance from the ESS installation, and access to a standpipe is necessary in an approved location.

1207.9.6

Open parking garages. ESS and

associated equipment that are located in open

parking garages shall comply with all of the

following:

1. ESS shall not be located within 15 m of air inlets for building HVAC systems.

Exception: This distance shall be permitted to be

reduced to 7.5 m if the automatic fire alarm system monitoring the radiant-energy sensing detectors deenergizes the ventilation system connected to the air intakes upon detection of fire.
2. ESS shall not be located within 7.5 m of exits leading from the attached building where located on a covered level of the parking structure not directly open to the sky above.
3. An approved fence with a locked gate or other approved barrier shall be provided to keep the general public at least 1.5 m from the outer enclosure of the ESS.
❖ A fire involving an ESS installation in an open parking garage has the ability of exposing occupants both in the parking garage and in surrounding or connected buildings via the HVAC system. Therefore, proper distances from HVAC systems and exits are required. Where HVAC systems are designed to shut down based on the activation of radiant-energy sensing detectors monitoring the ESS, the separation is permitted to be cut in half to 7.5 m.
In order to reduce the likelihood of damage to the ESS or injury to occupants, the installation needs to be properly protected with a fence or other approved method.
1207.10Mobile ESS equipment and operations. Mobile ESS equipment and operations shall comply with Sections 1207.10.1 through 1207.10.7.7.
❖ This section addresses two types of mobile ESS installations/operations: (1) charging and storage of the mobile ESS at its home facility when it is not deployed to an event or facility and (2) deployment of the mobile ESS for temporary energy storage applications, such as providing power at an electric vehicle event. Mobile ESS charging and storage locations are treated the same as a stationary indoor or outdoor installation in accordance with Section 1207.7 or 1207.8, but it can include temporary electrical and fire suppression system connections. This provides an acceptable level of protection based on the exposures at the facility and prevents parties from using an ESS on wheels as a permanent ESS with less than effective protection.
This section also includes requirements for deploying mobile ESS to a facility or event to provide up to 30 days of temporary power (with some exceptions). An operational permit is required for each mobile ESS deployment.

Note a references Section 1207.10.2 for information on what is meant by “deployment.” Note b acknowledges that it is not practical to regulate wheeled vehicles and trailers for seismic and structural requirements. Note c allows the water supply to be a temporary connection. Note d provides an exception for mobile ESS in deployment. Walk-up units do not need to meet the spacing-fromwall requirement of Section 1207.5.1. This is similarly allowed in outdoor installations located within 30 m of exposures. Note e recognizes the temporary nature of the use and does not require that alarm signals be transmitted to an approved location.

1207.10.1 Charging and storage. For the purpose of Section 1207.10, charging and storage covers the operation where mobile ESS are charged and stored so they are ready for deployment to another site, and where they are charged and stored after a deployment.

❖ This section describes when such units are not deployed and are being charged and stored. When mobile ESS are charging and stored, they are regulated as applicable to the way in which they are stored. The applicability of the installation types is addressed by Section 1207.10.6.

1207.10.2 Deployment. For the purpose of Section 1207.10, deployment covers operations where mobile ESS are located at a site other than the charging and storage site and are being used to provide power.

❖ When the mobile ESS is in use, it is considered as deployed and all the requirements of Table 1207.10 are applicable versus the requirements found in Sections 1207.7 through 1207.9.

1207.10.3 Permits. Construction and operational permits shall be provided for charging and storage of mobile ESS and operational permits shall be provided for deployment of mobile ESS as required by Section 1207.1.2.

❖ This section addresses permits for both aspects of ESS used for mobile applications. When mobile ESS are charging and in storage, both construction and operational permits are required. When deployed, operational permits are needed for that function.

1207.10.4 Construction

documents.

Construction documents complying with Section

1207.1.3 shall be provided with the construction

permit application for mobile ESS charging and storage locations.
❖ When mobile ESS are being charged and stored, all the requirements found in Section 1207.1.3 for construction documents must be provided with the construction permit application, which is no different than for other ESS installations.
1207.10.4.1 Deployment documents. The following information shall be provided with the operation permit applications for mobile ESS deployments:
1. Relevant information for the mobile ESS equipment and protection measures in the construction documents required by Section 1207.1.3.
2. Location and layout diagram of the area in which the mobile ESS is to be deployed, including a scale diagram of all nearby exposures.
3. Location and content of signage, including no smoking signs.
4. Description of fencing to be provided around the ESS, including locking methods.
5. Details on fire suppression, smoke and automatic fire detection, system monitoring, thermal management, exhaust ventilation and explosion control, if provided.
6. For deployment, the intended duration of operation, including anticipated connection and disconnection times and dates.
7. Location and description of local staging stops during transit to the deployment site. See Section 1207.10.7.5.
8. Description of the temporary wiring, including connection methods, conductor type and size, and circuit overcurrent protection to be provided.
9. Description of how fire suppression system connections to water supplies or extinguishing agents are to be provided.
10. Contact information for personnel who are responsible for maintaining and servicing the equipment, and responding to emergencies as required by Section 1207.1.6.1.
❖ Where the mobile ESS is deployed, additional information is required for the operational permit for such use. There are various aspects that are deployment specific that may affect the safety of the temporary installation. Some of

SBC 801-CC-2024

1037

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

these include the need to describe how fencing and associated locks will be provided or the location and layout of the area where it will be deployed. This information is in addition to that required by Section 1207.1.3.
1207.10.5 Approved locations. Locations where mobile ESS are charged, stored and deployed shall be restricted to the locations established on the construction and operational permits.
❖ Mobile ESS charging, storage and deployment locations may vary as long as all locations are those already established through the permitting process. Locations other than those that have been approved could pose safety risks as site and safety features have not been fully reviewed. Additionally, fire department personnel have no way of knowing about possible risks they may encounter when responding to an emergency at a nonpermitted site.
1207.10.6 Charging and storage. Installations where mobile ESS are charged and stored shall be treated as permanent ESS indoor or outdoor installations, and shall comply with the following sections, as applicable:
1. Indoor charging and storage shall comply with Section 1207.7.
2. Outdoor charging and storage shall comply with Section 1207.8.
3. Charging and storage on rooftops and in open parking garages shall comply with Section 1207.9.
Exceptions:
1. Electrical connections shall be permitted to be made using temporary wiring complying with the manufacturer’s instructions, the UL 9540 listing and SBC 401.
2. Fire suppression system connections to the water supply shall be permitted to use approved temporary connections.
❖ This section essentially establishes that when mobile ESS are charging or in storage, they should be dealt with using the scoping of Sections 1207.7 through 1207.9. In other words, they are no different than other applications of ESS. Once they are deployed, then Table 1207.10 and special permitting is required.
The exceptions recognize that these types of ESS are intended to be mobile and provide some relief from full compliance with Sections 1207.7 through 1207.9. When not deployed,

mobile ESS can use temporary wiring that complies with SBC 401 and UL 9540 and temporary connections to water supplies for fire protection.
1207.10.7 Deployed mobile ESS requirements. Deployed mobile ESS equipment and operations shall comply with this section and Table 1207.10.
❖ Once mobile ESS are deployed, Sections 1207.10.7.1 through 1207.10.7.7 are applicable. When deployed, there are unique risks that must be addressed that are site and application specific.
1207.10.7.1 Duration. The duration of mobile ESS deployment shall not exceed 30 days.
Exceptions:
1. Mobile ESS deployments that provide power for durations longer than 30 days shall comply with Section 1207.10.6.
2. Mobile ESS deployments shall not exceed 180 days unless additional operational permits are obtained.
❖ The deployment of mobile ESS are critical after natural disasters such as earthquakes but are not intended to replace permanent power sources. This section limits how long such mobile ESS can be used. The main code section limits deployments to 30 days; however, the exceptions address methods of extending that duration for much longer.
Exception 1 essentially requires the mobile ESS to be treated as an indoor, outdoor or special application ESS installation. As discussed in the commentary to Section 1207.10.6, there are allowances for wiring and fire protection connections that would apply to mobile ESS that are being stored or charged. Although Section 1207.10.6 allows rooftop and open parking garages for the storing and charging of mobile ESS, Section 1207.10.7.2 would not allow a deployed mobile ESS on a rooftop or in a covered parking garage.
Exception 2 allows deployment of not more than 180 days with extensions. Any deployment over 180 days would require additional operational permits.
1207.10.7.2 Restricted locations. Deployed mobile ESS operations shall not be located indoors, in covered parking garages, on rooftops, below grade or under building overhangs.
❖ This section provides several locations where mobile ESS cannot be deployed. Deployment at these locations creates potential exposure

SBC 801-CC-2024

1038

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

hazards to buildings, occupants and fire fighters. These installations, though well regulated, are not as permanent as other ESS and are at a higher risk of being damaged and possibly leading to fire and explosion hazards.
1207.10.7.3 Clearance to exposures. Deployed mobile ESS shall be separated by a minimum of 3 m from the following exposures:
1.Public ways. 2.Buildings. 3.Stored combustible materials. 4.Hazardous materials. 5.High-piled storage. 6.Other exposure hazards. Deployed mobile ESS shall be separated by a minimum of 15 m from public seating areas and from tents, canopies and membrane structures with an occupant load of 30 or more.
❖ This section uses separation to reduce risk from exposure hazards of a mobile ESS failure and resulting fire or explosion. Likewise, a failure of the ESS should not pose a hazard to surrounding exposures. This section is similar to Section 1207.8.3 for outdoor installations where a minimum of 3 m is required to exposures. A key difference is mobile ESS installations can be used for many types of temporary applications. The last sentence addresses a minimum separation distance of 15 m for public seating as well as tents and canopies with occupant loads over 30.
1207.10.7.4 Electrical connections. Electrical connections shall be made in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions and the UL 9540 listing. Temporary wiring for electrical power connections shall comply with SBC 401. Fixed electrical wiring shall not be provided.
❖ This section ensures that manufacturer’s instructions are used and that temporary wiring complies with SBC 401. Deployed mobile ESS are not intended for fixed use so they should not have fixed electrical wiring.
1207.10.7.5 Local staging. Mobile ESS in transit from the charging and storage location to the deployment location and back shall not be parked within 30 m of an occupied building for more than 1 hour during transit, unless specifically approved by the Fire official when the permit is issued.
❖ Mobile ESS in transit are at risk due to the energy contained. To avoid the risk to life safety of occupants in surrounding buildings, this section requires a 30 m separation where an ESS will be located for a longer period of time. A similar concept is used for other

hazardous materials regulated by this code, such as for LP-gas. In addition, it would be difficult to provide an operational permit each time a mobile ESS was in transit.
1207.10.7.6 Fencing. An approved fence with a locked gate or other approved barrier shall be provided to keep the general public at least 1.5 m from the outer enclosure of a deployed mobile ESS.
❖ This section protects the ESS from damage and provides some separation to protect the general public from exposure to the ESS. Outdoor installations are much more subject to vandalism and impact.
1207.10.7.7 Smoking. Smoking shall be prohibited within 3 m of mobile ESS. Signs shall be posted in accordance with Section 310.
❖ Regulating smoking near a deployed mobile ESS installation is key to reducing the likelihood of fire or explosion hazards.
1207.11ESS in Group R-3 and R-4 occupancies. ESS in Group R-3 and R-4 occupancies shall be installed and maintained in accordance with Sections 1207.11.1 through 1207.11.9. The temporary use of an owner or occupant’s electricpowered vehicle as an ESS shall be in accordance with Section 1207.11.10.
❖ ❖ An increased number of ESS utilizing stationary storage batteries are appearing on the market to help meet society’s growing energy demands. There are several driving factors, but a move to NetZero and energy independence are likely the biggest. This section does not mandate that ESS be provided but instead provides safety requirements that need to be applied if such systems are to be installed. It should be noted that Section 1207.11 is very similar to SBC 1101/1102 Section 328 for one- and two-family dwellings.
The definition of “Energy storage system” in Chapter 2 provides the code user with information on battery storage systems.
1207.11.1 Equipment listings. ESS shall be listed and labeled in accordance with UL 9540. ESS listed and labeled solely for utility or commercial use shall not be used for residential applications.
Exceptions:
1. 1.Where approved, repurposed unlisted battery systems from electric vehicles are allowed to be installed outdoors or in detached dedicated cabinets located not less

SBC 801-CC-2024

1039

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

-

than 1500 mm from exterior walls, property lines and public ways.
2. 2.ESS less than 1 kWh (3.6 megajoules).
❖ This section provides for the basic listing requirements of UL 9540, Energy Storage Systems and Equipment. This listing standard provides construction and performance requirements for investigating and listing stationary storage battery systems. This standard evaluates the ability of the ESS to operate under both normal operating conditions and certain fault conditions.
There are two exceptions to the application of Section 1207.11.1. Since ESS are a new, evolving technology, Exception 1 allows for installations of repurposed, nonlisted ESS from electric vehicles. A 1.5 m separation distance from exterior walls, the property line and public ways is required to mitigate the performance of the equipment under fault conditions that were not determined as part of a listing investigation. Installations utilizing ESS that are integral to electric vehicles are also allowed, provided that they comply with SBC 401 requirements that specifically cover such installations. See the commentary to SBC 1101/1102 Section 328.10.
Exception 2 exempts battery systems under 1 kWh, which is slightly greater than two 12V, 40 Ah batteries. This exempts common household standby power systems for tools, alarm systems and other appliances from having to comply with this section
Note that SBC 1101/1102 Section 328 exempts UL 9540-listed ESS that will not go into thermal runaway or produce flammable gas when subject to the UL 9540A Cell Level test. These ESS units must be labeled as “for use in residential dwelling units.”
1207.11.2 Installation. ESS shall be installed in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions and their listing.
❖ This section provides the enabling language to require that ESS are installed in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions and the listing.
1207.11.2.1 Spacing. Individual units shall be separated from each other by at least 900 mm of spacing unless smaller separation distances are documented to be adequate based on large-scale fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5.
❖ Regardless of whether the ESS complies with the listing and manufacturer’s instructions, this section requires minimal separation of units to

reduce the risk of a larger failure if one should occur. This separation distance is consistent with that required in SBC 1101/1102 for such installations. There is an allowance provided to reduce separations where large-scale fire testing demonstrates that the fire will not propagate between units.
1207.11.3 Location. ESS shall be installed only in the following locations:
1. Detached garages and detached accessory structures.
2. Attached garages separated from the dwelling unit living space and sleeping units in accordance with Section 406.3.2 of SBC 201.
3. Outdoors on exterior walls located a minimum of 900 mm from doors and windows.
4. Utility closets and storage or utility spaces within dwelling units and sleeping units.
❖ This section sets out specific allowable locations for ESS installations that are intended to reduce the risk to occupants where they are most at risk in their homes. Note that SBC 1101/1102 Section 328.4 specifically prohibits such units in sleeping rooms and spaces that are directly associated with such rooms. People are most at risk where they sleep. This section does not specifically state this, but it is within the intent to prohibit ESS in such locations. Minimally, Item 4 of this section allows such units only within utility closets and storage and utility spaces within dwelling and sleeping units.
1207.11.4 Energy ratings. Individual ESS units shall have a maximum rating of 20 kWh. The aggregate rating structure shall not exceed:
1. 40 kWh within utility closets and storage or utility spaces.
2. 80 kWh in attached or detached garages and detached accessory structures.
3. 80 kWh on exterior walls.
4. 80 kWh outdoors on the ground.
❖ This section sets limitations on the maximum energy rating of individual units as well as an aggregate energy rating for specific installation locations. The size of an event is directly correlated to the amount of energy stored. Where more energy rating is desired, the installation shall be in accordance with the rest of Section 1207.

SBC 801-CC-2024

1040

-

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS

1207.11.5 Electrical installation. ESS shall be installed in accordance with SBC 401. Inverters shall be listed and labeled in accordance with UL 1741 or provided as part of the UL 9540 listing. Systems connected to the utility grid shall use inverters listed for utility interaction.
❖ Compliance with SBC 401 is crucial. Additionally, inverters that are often associated with energy storage and PV installation to convert DC power to AC power must be listed and labeled. This section requires either listing with UL 1741 or UL 9540 where the inverters are part of the listing. In some cases, systems feed energy back to the grid, so the inverters need to be listed for that application as well.
1207.11.6 Fire detection. Rooms and areas within dwellings units, sleeping units and attached garages in which ESS are installed shall be protected by smoke alarms in accordance with Section 907.2.11. A heat detector listed and interconnected to the smoke alarms shall be installed in locations within dwelling units, sleeping units and attached garages where smoke alarms cannot be installed based on their listing.
❖ For early warning of an event, this section requires that an interconnected smoke alarm system with a smoke alarm be installed in the room or space where the ESS is located. If the space is not conducive to the installation of a smoke alarm, a listed heat alarm can be installed and interconnected to the smoke alarm system so that the occupants will be alerted to activation. Smoke alarms or heat alarms, where applicable, are generally going to be required in most locations listed in this section, as required in Section 907.2.11. However, additional detectors may be required based on the specific location of the ESS installation.
1207.11.7 Protection from impact. Stationary storage battery systems installed in a location subject to vehicle damage shall be protected by approved barriers. Appliances in garages shall also be installed in accordance with Section 304.3 of SBC 501.

❖ To reduce the risk of a failure of an ESS unit due to impact, impact protection is required. Impact could create a situation where thermal runaway occurs. This is addressed for appliances in SBC 501 and is referenced for more detailed requirements.
1207.11.8 Ventilation. Indoor installations of ESS that include batteries that produce hydrogen or other flammable gases during charging shall be provided with exhaust ventilation in accordance with Section 1207.6.1.
❖ This section requires ventilation be provided for indoor installations of ESS technologies, such as those including lead-acid batteries that are capable of producing hydrogen gas during charging.
1207.11.9 Toxic and highly toxic gas. ESS that have the potential to release toxic or highly toxic gas during charging, discharging and normal use conditions shall not be installed within Group R-3 or R-4 occupancies.
❖ This section simply prohibits the use of any ESS that have any potential for generating or releasing toxic or highly toxic gases. Flammable gases are addressed through ventilation in Section 1207.11.8.
1207.11.10 Electric vehicle use. The temporary use of an owner or occupant′s electricpowered vehicle to power a dwelling unit or sleeping unit while parked in an attached or detached garage or outside shall comply with the vehicle manufacturer′s instructions and SBC 401.
❖ This section provides for the temporary use of an electric vehicle as an ESS to power the dwelling, provided that it is done in compliance with SBC 401 and the manufacturer′s instructions. Requiring compliance with the manufacturer′s instructions ensures that only electric vehicles designed and manufactured for use as an ESS are utilized compared to someone adding nonapproved electrical connections to an electric vehicle that is not designed for this purpose.
